chunk_summary: Eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE) diagnosis requires endoscopy with biopsies, targeting visible lesions and obtaining at least six samples. Histologic thresholds include ≥15 eosinophils/HPF. Symptom improvement alone is insufficient for remission assessment or food trigger identification.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical presentation and age-dependent symptoms of EoE in children.
Learn the diagnostic workflow for EoE, including endoscopy and histologic thresholds.
Recognize the importance of biopsy confirmation to differentiate EoE from reflux disorders.
Explore the limitations of symptom-based remission and allergy testing in EoE management.
key_facts: EoE is a chronic immune-mediated esophageal disease with symptoms like dysphagia, vomiting, and food impaction.
Diagnosis requires upper endoscopy with at least six biopsies from proximal and distal esophagus.
Histologic threshold for EoE diagnosis is ≥15 eosinophils per high power field (HPF).
Symptom severity does not reliably correlate with tissue inflammation in EoE.
Allergy testing should not guide elimination diets for EoE management.
Early referral to pediatric endoscopy centers is critical, especially in resource-limited settings like India.
reference_quote: Diagnosis requires upper endoscopy with esophageal biopsies, even when the mucosa looks normal, because a normal-appearing esophagus does not exclude disease.
document_key_topics: EoE diagnostic endoscopy and biopsy protocols
Limitations of symptom-based remission in EoE
Role of early referral pathways in pediatric EoE management
